learn.title: Solar Panel Types
learn.intro: Solar panels are key to clean energy, but not all panels are equal. Choosing between monocrystalline, polycrystalline, and thin-film affects efficiency, cost, and suitability for specific environments. This matters as solar scales up globally to replace fossil fuels.
learn.c1_text: Monocrystalline panels use single-crystal silicon, offering high efficiency (15-22%) and durability. They’re ideal for limited spaces but costlier. For example, a 1kW monocrystalline system might cost ₹1.5 lakh in India, making it pricier but space-efficient.
learn.c2_text: Polycrystalline panels use multiple silicon fragments, slightly less efficient (13-16%) but cheaper. They’re budget-friendly for large installations. A 1kW polycrystalline system could cost ₹1.2 lakh, offering a balance of cost and performance for rural setups.
learn.c3_text: Thin-film panels are lightweight and flexible, with lower efficiency (10-13%). They perform better in heat and cloudy conditions. India’s solar farms increasingly use thin-film for cost-effective large-scale projects, though they need more space.
learn.stat1: India: 60% of solar farms use thin-film for utility-scale projects (2023 NREL data)
learn.stat2: Monocrystalline panels cost 20-30% more than polycrystalline (NREL)
learn.stat3: Thin-film requires 2x land area compared to crystalline panels (NREL)

learn.title: Agrivoltaics
learn.intro: Agrivoltaics merges solar farms with farming, solving land conflict. By placing panels above crops, it generates power while boosting yields. This dual-use approach is vital for sustainable land use in India’s agrarian economy.
learn.c1_text: Agrivoltaics allows crops to grow under solar panels. Studies show 10-20% higher crop yields due to shade and moisture retention. For instance, tomatoes grown under panels in Germany saw a 30% yield increase.
learn.c2_text: Panels are spaced to allow sunlight penetration. In India, rice paddies under solar arrays in Gujarat have improved irrigation efficiency by 25%, reducing water use while generating 500 MW annually.
learn.c3_text: India’s agrivoltaics pilot in Rajasthan combines solar with dairy farming. Farmers earn ₹50,000/year from solar and ₹30,000 from crops, proving economic viability. The MNRE supports this with subsidies.
learn.stat1: India: 10 MW agrivoltaics installed (2024 MNRE data)
learn.stat2: Global land savings: 30% when combining solar and farming (IRENA)
learn.stat3: Rajasthan pilot: 50% crop insurance coverage under panels (MNRE)
learn.stat4: Agrivoltaics can reduce water use by 40% in arid regions (IRENA)

learn.title: Green Hydrogen Production
learn.intro: Green hydrogen, produced via water electrolysis using renewables, is a clean fuel for industry and transport. Comparing PEM (Proton Exchange Membrane) and alkaline electrolyzers is critical as hydrogen aims to replace fossil fuels in hard-to-decarbonize sectors.
learn.c1_text: PEM uses a solid polymer membrane, offering high efficiency (60-70%) and fast response. It’s ideal for variable renewables like solar. A 1 MW PEM system in India costs ₹1.8 crore, suitable for industrial hydrogen demand.
learn.c2_text: Alkaline uses liquid electrolyte, with lower efficiency (50-60%) but cheaper upfront costs. It’s better for large, steady production. India’s NTPC is testing 500 MW alkaline plants for grid-scale hydrogen.
learn.c3_text: PEM costs ₹15-20/kg hydrogen, while alkaline is ₹10-15/kg. However, PEM’s efficiency justifies higher costs in urban areas. IRENA projects PEM adoption in India could cut hydrogen prices by 30% by 2030.
learn.stat3: PEM market growth: 40% CAGR in India (2024 MNRE)
learn.stat4: Hydrogen demand in India: 10 MTPD by 2030 (MNRE)

learn.title: Solar PV Electricity
learn.intro: Solar panels convert sunlight into electricity via the photoelectric effect. When photons hit silicon, they release electrons, creating current. This process powers homes and grids, making solar a cornerstone of renewable energy.
learn.c1_text: Sunlight excites electrons in silicon cells, generating DC electricity. A single panel might produce 300-400Wh/day in India. For example, a 300W panel in Delhi generates ~1.5 kWh/month.
learn.c2_text: Inverters convert DC to AC for home use. Grid-tied systems feed excess power back. India’s grid now accepts 15 GW of rooftop solar, reducing peak demand by 8% in urban areas.
learn.c3_text: India’s solar rooftop program targets 40 GW by 2030. In Rajasthan, households save ₹12,000/year on electricity bills. The US DOE notes rooftop solar could meet 40% of India’s urban demand.
learn.stat1: India: 15 GW rooftop solar installed (2024 US DOE)
learn.stat4: Solar accounts for 10% of India’s electricity (2024 MNRE)

learn.title: Levelised Cost of Energy
learn.intro: LCOE compares the average cost of generating energy over a project’s lifetime. It’s essential for policymakers to choose cost-effective renewables. Lower LCOE means more investment in solar/wind over coal or gas.
learn.c1_text: LCOE calculates total costs (installation, maintenance) divided by energy produced. A solar plant with ₹500 crore investment and 10 GW output has an LCOE of ₹3/kWh. This helps compare solar vs fossil fuels.
learn.c2_text: LCOE depends on tech efficiency, fuel costs, and lifespan. Wind has LCOE ₹2-4/kWh, while coal is ₹5-8/kWh. IRENA reports solar LCOE dropped 90% since 2010, making it the cheapest.
learn.c3_text: India’s solar LCOE is ₹2.4/kWh (MNRE 2024), cheaper than gas at ₹4.5/kWh. The PLI scheme reduces manufacturing costs, further lowering LCOE. This drives solar adoption over diesel generators.
learn.stat4: India’s PLI reduces solar panel costs by 15% (MNRE)

learn.title: India’s Solar PLI Scheme
learn.intro: India’s Production Linked Incentive (PLI) for solar manufacturing boosts local production, reducing import dependence. By subsidizing panel makers, it lowers costs and creates jobs, aligning with India’s 500 GW renewable target by 2030.
learn.c1_text: PLI offers financial incentives to domestic solar manufacturers. Companies get ₹5,000-10,000 per kW produced. This has spurred 20+ new factories in India, cutting panel prices by 15% since 2022.
learn.c2_text: PLI reduces Indian solar panel costs from ₹80/kW to ₹65/kW. A 5 MW plant now costs ₹3.25 crore vs ₹4.5 crore earlier. This makes solar competitive with diesel in rural areas.
learn.c3_text: India aims to produce 100 GW of solar panels domestically by 2030 via PLI. States like Gujarat host 60% of new factories. The scheme has created 50,000 jobs in manufacturing and R&D.
